name: model_theory
assumptions: A model is an interpretation that satisfies a given set of sentences in a formal language
The relationship between syntax (formal theories) and semantics (models) is fundamental to understanding representation
Model-theoretic techniques apply beyond mathematics: to databases, software specification, knowledge representation, and natural language semantics
Different logics (first-order, second-order, modal, intuitionistic) give rise to different model-theoretic frameworks

norms.message: Should specify the satisfaction relation between sentences and models
Should distinguish between semantic consequence and syntactic provability
Should consider whether theories uniquely determine their models
Should analyse what can and cannot be expressed in a given logical language

classic_texts: Tarski, 'The Concept of Truth in Formalized Languages'
Gödel, 'The Completeness of the Axioms of the Functional Calculus of Logic'

bridge_warnings.explanation: Model-theoretic semantics maps syntax to mathematical structures via truth conditions; database models represent data schemas; cognitive models represent mental processes. These are distinct senses of 'model' with different success criteria.

Tarskian logical consequence is a semantic relation; program execution is an operational process. Curry-Howard provides a bridge but the normative standards differ: truth-preservation vs termination/correctness.
